text: CINQUEFRONDI, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 1ma, 15 m. ENE of Palmi. Pop. 4,552. This town was nearly ruined by an earthquake in 1783.

CINTEGABELLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Muret.—The cant. comprises 6 com.—The com. and town, on the r. bank of the Ariége, 21 m. S of Toulonse, had a pop. of 4,016 in 1841.

CINTRA, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, comarca of Alemquer, 12 m. NW of Lisbon, on the slope of the Serra-de-C., which, detaching itself from the Monte-Junto to the W of Santarem, runs SW to the coast, on which it terminates in the low cliff called the Rock of Lisbon. The village is delightfully situated at the foot of a hill whose base is thickly clothed with wood, while its summit is crowned with loose crags and sharp pinnacles. Ivy-clad walls covered at top with amaryllises and crimson geraniums, and huge evergreen oaks and cork-trees intertwined with vines, give additional beauty to the scene. It is surrounded by the scattered quintas or summer-residences of the nobles and the wealthier citizens and English residents of Lisbon; but in itself, and though forming the Richmond of Lisbon, C. is a poor place, with no attractions beyond its scenery. The ancient royal palace of Ramalhão is a singular edifice, of which Beckford says, "the Alhambra itself is scarcely more morisco in point of architecture than this confused pile, which seems to grow out of the summit of a rocky eminence." the hall of the Marialva palace here, was signed the famous convention of C. At Montserrat are the ruins of Mr. Beckford's once celebrated villa. Matthews compares C. with Malvern; "but," says he, "to the heights of Malvern must be added some hundred feet of perpendicular rock." The valley of Colares, at a short distance to the W of C., and opening to the Atlantic, is one of the richest and best cultivated spots in the kingdom, forming the fruit-garden for the supply of the capital.
CINTRA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, at the mouth of the Maracanan, in a fertile but ill cultivated district.
CINTRAY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Breteuil. Pop. 618.
CINTREY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Vitré. Pop. 418.

CINTRUENIGO, a town of Spain, in Navarre, in the prov. of Logrono, 2 m. S of Corella, on the r. bank of the Alhama. Pop. 2,000. It is an old town, and retains some traces of Roman and Moorish architecture.
CIOLA, a village of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 20 m. SE of Forli, 9 m. W of Rimini.
CIOPPE', a small river of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, rising in the Serra-de-C.; flowing N; and falling into the Atlantic 15 m. W of Fortaleza, near a village of the same name.

CIOTAT (LA), a canton, commune, and port of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, arrond. of Marseilles.—The cant. comprises 4 com., with a pop. in 1841 of 9,060.—The port is on the W coast of the little bay of Leques or La C., 15 m. SE of Marseilles, in N lat. 43° 10′ 27", E long. 5° 37'. The port is good, and can receive vessels of 800 tons. The town is well-built, and surrounded with walls. Pop. in 1841, 5,902. Its chief trade is ship and steam-boat building, and the traffic arising out of its commercial character as an entrepot for wine, oil, and fruits, and as an anchovy and sardine fishing station.
CIPIME'O, a small hacienda in the state of Michoacan, in Mexico, 13 leagues ESE of Zamora, on a stream of the same name. It was the scene of some important struggles in the war of independence.
CIPRIANO (SAN), a town of Naples, in Principato-Citra, in the district and 6 m. NE of Salerno. Pop. 1,460.
CIRAL, a village of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Carrouges, 10 m. NW of Alençon. Pop. 1,272.
CIRAUQUI, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, 9 m. SW of Pampeluna, near the l. bank of the Salado, in the partido of Estella.

CIRCARS (THE NORTHERN), an extensive province of Hindostan, on the W side of the bay of | Christians. 568 Bengal; between the 15th and 20th degrees of N lat., but little more than one degree in breadth. On the N it is bounded by Orissa; on the E and SE by the sea; on the S by the Carnatic; on the W by Gundwana; and on the NW by a range of hills which divides it from the territories of the rajah of Berar. From the earliest period with which we are acquainted, it has been divided into five districts called circars, viz.: Guntur or Mortezabad, Kondapille or Mustafabad, Ellore, Rajamendri, and Cicacole or Sikabol, called also Kalinga. In 1804 it was divided into the 5 collectorships of Gantur, Masulipatam, Rajamendri, Vizagapatam, and Ganjam, whose area and pop. in 1836-7, and revenue, are thus returned: Pop. Area. Revenue. Nearly one-half of the superficial area of this prov. is supposed capable of culture or pasture; the remainder consists of woods, water, barren hills, and a sand waste 3 m. broad bordering the whole sea - coast. The surface for above 35 m. inland is a level plain; beyond this lies a more elevated stripe about 15 m. in breadth; and beyond this, to the N of the Godaveri, a still more elevated district stretches to the great ridge of mountains on the Gundwana frontier. It is well-watered by numerous rivers, which, rising in the mountains on the NW, discharge themselves into the bay of Bengal. The Godaveri and Kistna rivers have also their mouths within this prov. The Chilka lake is the N limit of the district. In Masulipatam is the Colair lake; and several lagunes occur on the coast. The country-with the exception of the stripe along the coast already noticed—is fertile, and produces all kinds of grain,—but principally rice,-also tobacco, sugar, and cotton. It also furnishes very fine teak timber for ship-building. The domestic animals are sheep and horned cattle.—The climate to the N of the Godaveri differs considerably from that to the S. From the end of August to the beginning of November the rains are excessive on the N side of that river, with winds from the W. A temperate season with NE winds succeeds, and prevails till the vernal equinox. From that period to the beginning of the rains, the heat is excessive. On the S side of the river, S winds prevail after the termination of the rains. From March to June, a W wind blows, accompanied with intense heat. The Circars are inhabited by an ingenious and industrious race, who manufacture large quantities of salt, muslins, chintzes, calicoes, woollen carpets, silks, and other goods. The principal part of the Company's piece-goods were the produce of this district. Wax, salt, pepper, indigo, and tobacco are among the exports. Ships of 500 tons measure. ment have been built at Kosinga and Narsipur, the two principal mouths of the Godaveri; and about 50,000 tons of small craft are employed in the coasting trade. But notwithstanding its extensive line of sea-coast, extending 470 m., this district does not possess a harbour where a large vessel can lie at anchor, much less be sheltered from the storms. Besides the five towns which give name to the five districts above mentioned, this prov. contains the following places of consequence, viz.: Kalingapatam, Vizianagram, Bindipatam, and Masulipatam. The pop. was estimated in 1836-7 at 2,565,379, the greater part of whom are Hindus of the Telinga and Orissa race; the remainder, who are principally found in the towns, are Mahommedans, and a few native Till this prov. came into the hands of the English, it was badly governed. The zemindars or principal landholders were surrounded by military tenants, and in a very irregular state of subjection to the nizam; and not only the form, but even the remembrance of civil authority seems to have been lost. The collection of the revenues could only be effected by a military force; and a considerable force is still required to keep the mountaineers in check. The Hindu princes of Orissa seem to have maintained their independence till a comparatively recent period. The country was very imperfectly reduced till about the year 1571, during the reign of Ibrahim Kutub Shah of Golcondah. In the year 1687, this prov. fell, with the Golcondah empire, into the hands of Aurungzebe; and under the Mogul dynasty it constituted a part of the government of the nizam or subadar of the Deccan. In consequence of this right, the nabob Salobet Jang made an assignment of them to M. Bussy, in the year 1752-3, for the payment of the French auxiliary forces, by whom they were retained till 1759, when they were reconquered by the British for the nizam. In 1765, Lord Clive received a formal grant of them from Shah Alum, the Great Mogul; but as one of them, called the Gantur circar, had been settled on Barselat Jang, the brother of the nizam, he was allowed to retain it during his life; and it did not come into the possession of the British till 1788. The Company, however, agreed to pay the nizam an annual pescush or tribute of £70,000 for the ceded district; and this was redeemed in 1823 by a payment in full of about £200,000. In 1832 the revenue of the Circars amounted to 76,68,018 rupees, or £766,800. In 1836-7 it had increased to 83,05,057, or £830,050. This prov. is included in the Madras presidency.

CIRCASSIA, a name generally applied to that portion of the Caucasian provinces of Russia which is situated between the 43d and 45th parallels of N lat. and the 37th and 47th meridians of E long., comprising nearly the whole rugged mountainous country forming the N declivity of the Caucasian range, and stretching from the shores of the Black sea to the Wridge of the basin of the Caspian. The Terek and the Kuban, as far as their respective courses lie W and E, may be considered as tracing generally the N boundary of the C. territory, while the summit of the Caucasian chain forms its S limit.—The physical features of this country have been traced in the article CAUCASUS. The different provinces of C. may be regarded under three distinct divisions:-1st. Under the first of these are classed those districts whose inhabitants are continually at war with Russia, comprising the whole of the coast from Soukhoum-Kaleh to Anapa, of which the S part is called Abasia or Abassa; the northern is included in the prov. of Natukoitch; and to the E of this is the prov. of Shapsuk. The pop. of these districts is estimated to amount to 300,000; and they furnish out of this number at least 50,000 well-armed and well-mounted warriors. -2d. The next division includes all the lowland and neutral provs. on the Kuban, and its tributary, the Laba, occupying the plains between these streams and the mountains. The consequence of their physical position is, that having no defiles or impregnable positions to retreat upon, but living in extensive villages on the plains, the pop. of these districts have been peculiarly obnoxious to the hostility of Russia; and, after a long and sanguinary struggle, have been compelled to make peace, and remain neutral in the war which she still prosecutes against their neighbours. Their united pop. is as great as that of the belligerent districts.-The 3d division is the great prov. of Abbasak, a mountainous region, placed in the centre . CIRCASSIA. of the others, and whose pop., amounting to about 400,000, though avowedly hostile to Russia, are rarely, from their position, brought into collision with her troops.—A 4th division, consisting of Great and Little Kabarda, situate on the Kuban and the Terek, is no longer independent. Its inhabitants, though retaining their arms, give hostages to Russia, and allow a free passage to her troops through their territory. In the article Caucasus, a more detailed account is given of the different territories occupied by the independent Circassian tribes; the remainder of the present article will be devoted to a brief account of the people themselves. The C. Proper are located to the E of the Abassians, who appear to be a distinct nation, and to have long held exclusive possession of the NW part of the Caucasus. The C. term themselves Adigi, and by the Tartars are named Tcherkess, whence the name by which they are known in Europe. Klaproth says they have brown hair and eyes, long faces, thin straight noses, and elegant forms. Pallas pronounces them a handsome race of people, of tall stature, and thin form, but great muscular activity and strength. The dress of their warriors resembles that of the knights of chivalry. Their chiefs are covered with a coat of mail, with steel armlets, which secure the hands and arms from the elbows downwards. Their head is covered with a steel helmet, which is attached to the coat of mail, so that the whole forms a hood, which allows nothing to be seen but that part of the face between the eyebrows and the mouth. They carry in front, and on each side of the coat, a fluted pocket, containing ten or twelve wooden cases, which serve as cartridge - boxes; and their arms consist of a curved sabre without the guard, a dagger, a pistol, and an Albanian musket, or a bow. They mount their own arms; and the temper of their steel, and method of staining silver are inimitable. As to the other parts of their costume, they frequently vary their ornaments, and the cut of their clothes, as well as the shape of their caps. The C. matron, when dressed for effect, appears in a costly silk robe, open in front, and confined at the waist by a girdle exuberant of silver, muslin trousers of variegated colours, red slippers, a turban com posed of a shawl, the ends of which fall in graceful folds over the neck and shoulders, completely concealing the hair, a large gossamer veil that nearly envelopes her whole person, and a prodigal variety of massive trinkets, the fashion of which presents memorials of the connexion that once subsisted between these shores and the republics of Genoa and Venice. The dress of the unmarried females scarcely differs from that of the Tartar maidens. They all, of whatever age, wear tight leather corsets, from which they are not relieved until the day of their nuptials. [Quin.] Their villages, or rather hamlets, are generally, with a view to safety from invasion, concealed behind embankments and clusters of trees. So ingeniously are their cottages shaded from the eye, that a stranger would sometimes not suspect that he was in an inhabited country, were it not for the flocks and herds which he meets grazing, and the men and women he may see following their agricultural pursuits. These cottages are partly excavated in the earth, partly built of mud walls, and covered with flat roofs of earth and gravel, so firmly beat together that they are completely impenetrable to rain. In the fine season they spread a carpet and raise their divan on these roofs, and there take their meals and receive their friends. There too they place their grain and fruits to dry; and the ample chimney in the centre serves usually as the channel of communication between neighbours. It is, moreover, the only aperture through which the apartments below are lighted. Thus simple provision is made for warmth in winter, and for refreshing coolness in the heats of summer. The better order of habitations, however, rise considerably above the ground, and exhibit windows, and verandahs, and fronts plastered with a kind of mineral clay. Like many houses in the interior of Spain, they have no glass frames,-nothing, in fact, beyond simple square apertures, which are secured at night by wooden shutters. They have scarcely any furniture within, except a very plain divan, mats on the floor, pillows, and coverlets; but the walls of the common apartment usually display all the pride of the family, being lined with shields, armour, weapons of every kind, housings, and rich bridles. Here the females, who probably have been engaged in ploughing or reaping or making fences all the day, appear in the evening dressed out in gold brocade and silver muslin, and all the pomp of C. costume. [Quin.] With the exception of such arts as are connected with their equipment for the field, the C. exhibit little industry; and their rural economy is in a very rude state. A few wind-mills have recently been erected amongst them, but the grain is generally pounded to flour in mortars. The Turks supply them with cloth manufactured in Anatolia, and of which the annual importation amounts to about 2,500,000 piastres = £70,000, also salt, iron, steel, tin, gold and silver wire, morocco leather, arms, gunpowder, and hardware, to the value of about 500,000 piastres annually. The C. give in exchange wax, honey, salt provisions, skins, and furs, but chiefly slaves,-if that indeed may be called slavery in which all parties appear to be equally consenting to the contract. "The Turks obtain from Caucasus handsomer and healthier wives than those born in the harem; and the Circassian beauties are delighted to exchange the poverty and toil of their fathers' mountain huts for the luxurious far niente of the seraglio, of whose wonders and delights their ears are regaled, from childhood upwards, with the most glowing description. The trade, although greatly impeded and very hazardous, still goes on. Small Turkish craft creep up to the coast, cautiously evading the Russian cruisers, enter creeks and inlets, and are dragged by the C.s high and dry upon the beach, there to remain till the negotiation for their live cargo is completed, an operation that generally lasts a few weeks. The women sold are the daughters of serfs and freemen: rarely does a vork consent to dispose of his sister or daughter, although the case does sometimes occur. But, whilst the sale goes on, the slave-ships are anything but secure. It is a small matter to have escaped the Russian frigates and steamers. Each of the kreposts possesses a little squadron of row-boats, manned with Cossacks, who pull along the coast in search of Turkish vessels. If they detect one, they land in the night, and endeavour to set fire to it before the mountaineers can come to the assistance of the crew. The Turks, who live in profound terror of these Cossack coast-guards, resort to every possible expedient to escape their observation; often covering their vessels with dry leaves and boughs, and tying fir branches to the masts, that the scouts may take them for trees. If they are captured at sea by the cruisers, the crew are sent to hard labour in Siberia, and the C. girls are married to Cossacks, or divided as handmaidens amongst the Russian staff-officers. From 30 to 40 slaves compose the usual cargo of each of these vessels, which are so small that the poor creatures are packed almost like herrings in a barrel. But they patiently endure the misery of the voyage, in anticipation of the honeyed existence of the harem. It is calculated that one vessel out of six is taken or lost. In the winter of 1843-4, 28 ships left the coast of Asia Minor for that of Caucasia: 23 safely returned, 3 were burned by the Russians, and 2 swallowed by the waves." [Wagner.] The C. are socially divided into four classes:-the pshees, or princes,-the vorks, ouzdens, or nobles, -the tocars, slokothfles, or freemen, -and the serfs. The pshees are petty suzerains who appear to have been originally of Kabardian descent, and to have carried their arms into every part of C. It is the prisoners made in their wars who constitute the servile class. The will of the pshee is a law to his followers; and the booty he collects in his forays is the reward of their fidelity. In the plains and provinces of the Kuban, where there are large and flourishing villages, the authority of the pshees is more permanent than in the other provs, and acquires in consequence a more peaceful and patriarchal character. The second class, that of the vorks or nobles, seems to have contributed as little to the national peace and prosperity as the first, In wealth and power they are not paramount to the third class; but they are tenacious of their dignity, and never mingle their blood by marriage with the latter. The third class, or freemen, constitute the great body of the people. The fourth class consists of serfs or slaves; but serfage in the Caucasus exists under conditions very mild and favourable to its members. Land, tenements, and cattle are allotted to serfs; they retain one-half of the produce of their estates; they can also, when they please, demand to be transferred to other masters; or purchase, when they have the means, their own liberty. All, whether bond or free, are clothed, fed, and housed in the same manner; and are on a par as to mental acquirements. In the labours of the field, the dangers of war, and the festivities of the banquetting hall, even the noble, though his rank is marked by certain ceremonious observances, is the associate of the serf, and the independent spirit that animates the one exists in a scarcely less degree in the other. Yet the highest value is set on ancient blood, insomuch that "no man is deemed to be of noble blood whose family is ever known to have been ignoble, even though it may have given birth to several kings." It rarely happens that a boy receives his education under the paternal roof. A prince commits his son, when only a few days old, to the care of one of his nobles, and seldom sees him again till the time of the young man's marriage: "hence," says Klaproth, "results the utmost indifference between the nearest relations. A prince reddens with indignation when he is asked concerning the health of his wife and children,-makes no reply,and commonly turns his back on the inquirer in contempt." The C. are still only partially reclaimed from Paganism to Mahommedanism. Several of the Lesghian tribes profess Mahommedanism; others less civilized worship the sun, moon, trees, and stars. In the other Kuban provinces, Mahommedanism is of an early date, and universally diffused; but in the two provs. of Shapshuk and Natukoitch, and along the sea-coast, the whole population were idolaters at the end of last cent., and have only lately been converted to Mahommedanism by Turkish missionaries. In those two provinces, Islamism, though professed by all, is as yet in its duties and observances but slightly cultivated; the great majority are uncircumcised, and on many parts of the coast Pagan rites and sacrifices, though modified, have not been entirely suppressed, though blended in a singular manner with what appear to be traditional remains of Christianity. They acknowledge a Supreme Being, a mother of God, and several celestial powers of a secondary order, whom they call apostles. The forests are their temples, and a cross placed before a tree consecrates an altar before which they offer sacrifice. One of the elders of the community officiates as minister: standing bare-headed by the side of the cross, habited in the species of travelling-cloak commonly used by the Tartars and Circassians, in the manner of a Dalmatique, he commences the ceremony by a propitiatory sacrifice. The victim usually brought to him is a sheep or a goat; but on occasions of great solemnity it is an ox. He then takes a taper, which is at the foot of the altar, and burns a few hairs from the animal at each place where it is to be struck; pours a little bourn -a liquor prepared by fermenting millet flour in water-on its head; and, after a short prayer of consecration, it is immolated. The head of the victim, which is sacred to the deity, they fix on a pole at some distance from the altar; the skin belongs to the officiating priest, who supplies the taper; the animal is purchased by the community jointly; and the remainder is dressed during the service, for the repast of the assembly. Several youths-for the most part slaves of the elder who officiates as priest-stand behind him, with cups of bouza and slices of bread. As soon as the sacrifice is completed, the priest, taking a piece of bread in one hand, and a cup in the other, raises them towards heaven, and invokes the Supreme Being to be favourable to them; he then blesses the viands, and presents them to the eldest of the company, who cats them. The assistants supply him with another cup, and a fresh piece of bread; and he supplicates the Mother of God, repeating the same ceremonial as he presents them to another of the eldest persons present. The ceremony is thus continued, addressing a separate prayer to each of their apostles, whom they thus reverence in succession. Before concluding, the minister announces a day for holding the next festival, which he appoints at his discretion; but it ought to take place once a-week, either on a Saturday, Sunday, Monday, or Tuesday, but never on any other day. After this, the repast is spread, which consists, besides the animals sacrificed, of other meat brought by each family assisting at the ceremony, as well as pasta, or millet boiled to paste, and liquor [Tausch]. Besides these feasts, the Circussians celebrate several others; and towards the end of October they keep a commemoration of the dead, in which they recommend their deceased friends to the protection of the saints. Bonza is the ordinary drink, but wine is made, and freely used even by such as profess Mahommedanism. The influence of Mahommedanism in C. is not only moral but political. It is Mahommedanism which makes the Caucasus so formidable a barrier against Russian encroachment, and that unites the C. tribes in the feelings and sympathies of a common faith to Turkey, even after the latter has withdrawn her protection and countenance. In their declaration of independence, which appeared a few years since, the C. say, or are made to say probably by the mouth of European diplomacy: "Our mountains have been the ramparts of Persia and Turkey,—they will become, unless supported, the gate to both,-they are now the only shelter for both. They are the doors of the house, by closing which alone the hearth can be defended. But, moreover, our blood, Circassian blood, fills the veins of the sultan. His mother, his harem, is Circassian. His slaves are Circassian. His ministers and his generals are Circassian. He is the chief of our faith, and also of our race; he possesses our hearts, and we offer him our allegiance; by all these ties we claim from him countenance and support; and if he will not or cannot defend his children and his subjects, let him think of the khans of the Crimea, whose descendant is among us. . . . We are 4,000,000, but we have unfortunately been divided into many tribes, languages, and creeds; we have various customs, traditions, interests, alliances, and feuds. We have hitherto never had one purpose; but we have modes of government, and habits of submission and command. The chief chosen by each body during war is implicitly obeyed, and our princes and our elders govern according to the custom of each place with greater authority than in the great states around us; but from our wanting a common chief amongst ourselves, we, who have ruled throughout the East, have chosen always a foreign leader. We have thus voluntarily submitted to the dominion of the khans of the Crimea, and afterwards to the sultans of Constantinople. . . . We have lost the chiefs that formerly could have collected hundreds of thousands of men under their banners; but we are now at last united, all as one man, in hatred to Russia; 200,000 alone of our people have been subjected by her during this long contest, of the remainder not one has voluntarily served Russia. Many children have been stolen, and sons of nobles taken as hostages; but such as could recollect a country have made their escape. We have amongst us men who have been favoured and flattered and honoured by the Emperor, and who have preferred to that favour the dangers of their country. We have amongst us thousands of Russians, who prefer our barbarism to the civilization of their country. Russia has built forts on points of our territory, but they dare not venture beyond the reach of their guns; 50,000 Russians have lately made an inroad, and they have been beaten. It is by arms, not by words, that a country can be conquered. If Russia conquers us, it will not be by arms, but by cutting off our communications, and making use of Turkey and Persia as if they were already hers; by rendering the sea impassable, as if it were her own; by blockading our coast; by destroying not only our vessels, but those of other states which approach us; by depriving us of a market for our produce; by preventing us from obtaining salt, gunpowder, and other necessaries of war, which to us are necessaries of life; by depriving us of hope." One of the most remarkable institutions existing in C. is that of voluntary associations, by which the C. have sought defence against the violence of military chiefs, -associations whose sole bond of union is an oath imposing, in the absence of all other ties, obligations of a most sacred character. The members of these communities all regard each other as brothers, and their families are not allowed to intermarry. Every society incurs the responsibility of the crimes of its members, and claims the exclusive right of punishing them. There are penalties established for all crimes; and the claims are adjusted by a jury of 12 persons, 6 from each of the litigant societies, whose verdict must be unanimous. The penalty, when it has been arranged, is not paid by the criminal, and is not received by the injured party; it is contributed by, and distributed among, the respective societies. Revenge is thus disarmed by the reflection that it deprives a whole society of its claim to indemnity; and, in seeking the redress of individual wrong, interest and not passion is consulted. "Such," says Mr. Longworth, "are the societies of C.; formed, it is evident, not for warlike or political, but for social and judicial purposes. In this respect they entirely differ from the clans or tribes of other mountainous regions, who, living together and united under a chieftain, are the ready instruments of his ambition; whereas the members of the different C. societies live apart from each other, and acknowledge no leader. When a gathering takes place for an expedition or foray, the banners under which the various bands are arrayed are not those of the tribes, but of the particular districts or streams where the warriors reside, The nobles, instead of being the heads of them—as some writers imagine-have their own societies as well as the freemen,-all originally distinct, though sometimes connected by an oath. This sort of alliance is not uncommon. The society of nobles called Chipaquo is united to the powerful one of Tocaos named Natquo, and from this connection has acquired the ascendancy it enjoys in Natukoitch. It was a similar alliance with the Tocao society of Rubleh that formerly gave the abbots the same degree of influence in Shapsuk. The advantages enjoyed by the member of a rich and powerful society, and the disadvantage under which an individual labours who has the misfortune to belong to a poor and weak one, are such as no personal qualities can neutralize or make up for. Every man feels that for the payment or exactions of fines the resources of his society are his own, and in proportion to these is he respected by his neighbours. Nor is this all: agreeably with the liberal ideas that prevail here generally with respect to property, and which know hardly of any bounds among the members of a society, a man has a claim upon it for anything that he may stand in need of. For example, if in want of a wife, and too poor to buy one-for they are very expensive articles-his society raise a subscription for him. The wives of the members, however strictly they appropriate them during their lives, revert when they die to the joint-stock company, who dispose of them as they think proper. This is one out of the many particulars in which the C. law is at variance with the Koran, which gives every wife, although originally a slave, her liberty, being after her husband's death her own mistress, and free to choose another. In everything that relates to their laws and their administration the C. still look to their societies, nor will they permit Islamism, whatever their respect for its pretensions, to dedicate the one, or usurp the other. They allow its representatives, it is true, a seat in their tribunals; but the cadi, who presides over, or is called in rather as an assessor to their juries, exercises little control, but merely expounds the Mussulman law, which they adopt or reject as it suits their ideas and customs. . . I have already remarked, and to the reader who wishes to form a just idea of the state of C. it cannot be too often repeated, that the feature which distinguishes its tribes from those of every other country in the world is, that their members have no other bond of union but an oath confirmed by the daily experience of its advantages, and that they are dispersed over their provinces promiscuously with the members of other tribes. The causes of this segregation I do not pretend to explain at full; there are two, however, sufficiently obvious. The members of a tribe, no matter of how many families it may be composed— and in some instances there are many thousands—are not allowed to intermarry, a regulation which would prove highly embarrassing were they all resident in one district. Another reason which may induce them to separate is, that it is much easier to obtain justice from members of a different tribe than among themselves, there not existing the same combination to enforce it. We shall understand this better, when we remember that near relations make but indifferent neighbours, and that a tribe in C. is but a large family, whose disputes it would be the more difficult to arrange, that they acknowledge no head among themselves, and are therefore the better friends the more they live asunder. But, whatever may be the causes of their separation, the effect of it is to render them useless for warlike or national purposes. For military organization, local influences are of much more avail; and in the council as well as in the field, their power is unfelt, while that of the princes and nobles, being of a military and feudal origin, has its weight, though personal merit and qualification have even a still greater sway."

CIRCELLO, or CIRCEO (MONTE), a mountain of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 35 m. SW of Frosinone, and 11 m. WSW of Terracina, forming a promontory 1,722 ft. in height, opposite the islands of Ponza. It is the Circaum promontorium of the ancients. It is supposed by some writers to be the isle of Circe, described by Homer as an island; and the character of the soil on the northern base indicates its having once been joined to the continent.
CIRCIA (Punta), a headland of Sicily, on the SE coast, forming the W side of La Marza bay.

CIRCLEVILLE, a town, the cap. of Pickaway CIR co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 26 m. S of Columbus. Pop. in 1840, 2,972. It lies on the E bank of the Sciota river, and is intersected by the Ohio canal, which here forms a fine aqueduct. The streets run at right angles, and are regularly built. The surrounding district is fertile, and contains considerable water-power.
CIRCOU, a river of the Malay peninsula, in the district of Patani, which falls into the gulf of Siam, 20 m. WNW of Patani.
CIRE', a village of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Aigrefeuille, 9 m. N of Rochefort. Pop. 1,000.

CIRELLA, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, in the district and 25 m. NNW of Paola, and 35 m. NW of Cosenza, on the shore of the Tuscan sea. Pop. 300. It was formerly an episcopal town. The trade, which is considerable, consists in raisins, silk, wine, and oil. In the vicinity is a small island of the same name. CIRENCESTER—colloquially, CICETER—a borough, market-town, and parish in Gloucestershire, on the W bank of the Churn, 41 m. N from its confluence with the Severn. A branch of the Thames and Severn canal comes close to the town, and the Cheltenham and Great Western railway has a station on the W side of the town, 95 m. from London; 26 m. from Cheltenham; and 119 m. from Derby, through Birmingham.—Area of p. 5,900 acres. Pop. in 1801, 4,030; in 1841, 6,014.—The town has a very respectable appearance as a country-town. It consists of four principal, and a number of less considerable streets; several new streets have been recently built, and others are in progress. The streets generally are well-paved, and supplied with water. C. is celebrated for the salubrity of its atmosphere, and its general healthfulness.—The church is a magnificent Gothic structure, with an embattled tower 132 ft. high, a nave, side-aisles, a choir or chancel, and 5 chapels. - The Royal Agricultural college is a spacious and imposing structure, of the ornamented Elizabethan style, the principal front showing a façade of 190 ft. by 50 ft. in height, the centre relieved by a well-proportioned tower surmounted by an octangular turret, the whole rising 93 ft. from the lawn. It is situated on an elevated part of the experimental farm, of 700 acres, attached to the institution, about 1 m. from the town of C. The objects of the institution are to provide scientific instruction in all that pertains to agriculture. Students are only admissible upon the nomination of a proprietor, or donor of £30. From 14 to 18 is the age at which they are eligible to enter. The annual payment of each student is £30, with such charges as the council may fix for the library, museum, &c. Non-resident students of any age are allowed, on the recommendation of a proprietor, to attend the lectures, and avail themselves of the practical instruction, upon the same annual payment of £30. The present number of students is about 60.—The manufacture of woollen cloth was formerly carried on in C. to a great extent, having been established as early as Henry IV., who granted a charter to a body of weavers in this town. It was the first wool-market in the kingdom; but the trade, both in wool and woollen cloth, is now trifling. There is a small carpet manufactory, however, and a large cloth one, in the town. The parish extends beyond the limits of the old, but coincides with those of the new parliamentary borough. C. returns two members to parliament. The number of registered electors in 1847 was 478. It is one of the polling-places for the E division of Gloucestershire. The petty sessions for the seven hundreds of C. are held here. - Camden terms C. "a famous city of great antiquity, called by Ptolemy, Corinium; by Antonine, Durocornovium." It was a town of importance during the Roman domination, when it was the metropolis of the Dobuni, and the seat of a Roman colony. Scarcely a year passes without the discovery here of some vestige of the Roman occupation.

CIRES-LES-MELLO, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Neuilly-en- Thelle, on the r. bank of the Therain, 10 m. WNW of Senlis. Pop. 1,330. Merinoes and calicoes are manufactured here.
CIREY-SUR-BLAISE, or CIREY-LE-CHATEAU, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute- Marne, cant. of Doulevant, on the r. bank of the Blaise. Pop. 721. There are iron-forges here.
CIREY-LES-FORGES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Lorquin, on the r. bank of the Vezouze, 12 m. SW of Sarrebourg. Pop. 2,332. There are large glass-works here.
CIRGUES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot, cant. of La Tronquière. Pop. 1,480.
CIRGUES-DE-JORDANNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Aurillac. Pop. 1,236. It is noted for its butter and cheese.
CIRGUES-DE-MALBERT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Cernin, 12 m. N of Aurillac. Pop. 1,471.
CIRGUES-LA-ROCHE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Servières, 24 m. SE of Tulle. Pop. 1,051.

CIRIA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 35 m. ESE of Soria, partido of Agrada, near the r. bank of the Manubles. Pop. 440. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains a parish-church, a custom-house, and a bank. Its trade consists chiefly in cattle.
CIRIE, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 12 m. NNW of Turin, on a branch of the Stura. Pop. 4,600. It contains 3 churches, and several convents.
CIRINUELA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 26 m. WSW of Logrono, partido of Santo-Domingo-de-la-Calzada.

CIRO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, in the district and 28 m. NNW of Cotrone, on a hill about 3 m. from the shore of the Ionian sea. Pop. 2,934. It is surrounded by walls, and defended by a fortress; and contains 3 churches, an episcopal palace, and a seminary. It is noted as the birth-place of the astronomer Gigli. Manna is extensively cultivated in the environs.

CIRON, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Gabaret; crosses the SW corner of the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne; enters the dep. of the Gironde; and after a total course of about 60 m., in a generally NNW direction, falls into the Garonne near Barsac. The navigation of this river extends to Corvette, a little above Villandraut. Faggots form its chief article of transit. Of these 18,000 steres are annually imported into Bordeaux.
CIRQ-LAPOPIE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot, cant. of Saint-Gery, on the l. bank of the Lot, 13 m. E of Cahors. Pop. 1,333.
CIRUELAS, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 10 m. NE of Guadalajara.—Also a valley in the same prov.
CIRUELAS-O'-VILLA-REAL, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 23 m. E of Toledo, partido of Ocana.
CIRUENA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 16 m. W of Logrono, partido and 3 m. E of Santo-Domingo-de-la-Calzada.
CIRUHECHES, a town of Spain, in New Castile, CIT in the prov. and 46 m. NE of Guadalajara, partido of Siguenza, on an affluent of the Henares.

CIRY-LE-NOBLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Toulon-sur-Arroux, 15 m. N of Charolles, on the r. bank of the Bourbince. Pop. 1,403. It contains mines of coal, and has manufactories of pottery, stoneware, and bricks.
CISANO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the delegation and 15 m. WNW of Verona, and district of Bardolino, near the E bank of Lake Garda. A fair is held here once a-year.
CISLA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 29 m. NNW of Avila, partido of Arevalo, on the r. bank of the Zapardiel.
CISLAGO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 16 m. NNW of Milan, district and 7 m. NE of Busto-Arsisio. Pop. 1,258.

CISMAR, a hamlet of Denmark, cap. of the amt of the same name, in the duchy of Holstein, 9 m. SE of Oldenburg, between the Baltic and the Grubersee. It contained an ancient Benedictine abbey, suppressed in 1544. The amt comprises an area of about 9 m., and contains 3 towns, 3 parishes, and 5,830 inhabitants.
CISMONE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 30 m. N of Vicenza, and 10 m. ENE of Asiago, at the junction of the Vanoi with the Brenta- Sugana. Pop. 2,000.
CISNA, a town of Galicia, in the circle, and 25 m. SSE of Sanok, at the foot of the Carpathian mountains, on the l. bank of the Solinka, an affluent of the San. In the environs is a mine of iron.
CISNEROS, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 25 m. NW of Palencia, partido of Frechilla. Pop. 1,241. It has a parish-church, and a customhouse.

CISOING, a village of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Templeuve-en-Pesvêle. Pop. 2,400. It has a spinning-mill. In the environs is an ancient abbey, in the park of which is a pyramid in memory of the battle of Fontenoy.
CIS-PLATINA. See BANDA - ORIENTAL and MONTE-VIDEO.
CISSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Pauillac. Pop. 933. The environs afford wine of excellent quality.
CISSE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. of Vouille, 8 m. NW of Poitiers. Pop. 1,012.

CISTERNA, a village of Naples, in the Terra-di- Lavoro, 6 m. W of Nola. Pop. 125. Volcanic tufa is quarried here.—Also a town in the Papal states, in the deleg. and 27 m. W of Frosinone. Pop. 2,500. Immense forests stretch from this place to the Mediterranean.—Also a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 9 m. W of Asti. Pop. 2,000.
CISTERNES-LAFORET, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Pontaumur. Pop. 1,000.
CISTERNINO, a town of Naples, in the Terra-di- Bari, 45 m. SE of Bari. Pop. 4,637.
CISTRIE'RES, a village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, cant. and 3 m. W of La-Chaise-Dieu. Pop. 1,029.
CITE' (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Perigueux. Pop. 1,926.

CITARA, a town of Naples, in Principato-Citra, 4 m. SW of Salerno, on the coast. CITARA, ZITARA, or QUIBDO, a town of New Grenada, the cap. of the prov. of Choco, on the r. bank of the Atrato, in a swampy district, 98 m. SW by S of Antioquia, and about 370 m. from the mouth of the river, which is navigable for vessels of 30 tons up to the town, and is joined at C. by the Quito river from the W. See articles ATRATO and CUPICA.
CITLAL-TEPETL. See ORIZABA.

CITEAUX, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, 15 m. NE of Beaune, in the cant. of Nuits, on the r. bank of the Vouge. Some remains still exist here of the celebrated abbey founded in 1098 by Robert, abbé of Molesme, in which San Bernard assumed the cowl in 1113.
CITOU, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Peyriac-Minervois, on the Argent-Double, 18 m. NE of Carcassone. Pop. 669.
CITTADELLA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 15 m. NE of Vicenza, on the l. bank of the Brentella, in N lat. 43° 38'. It is walled. Pop. 6,700.

CITTA'-DELLA-PIEVE, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 21 m. WSW of Perugia, on an eminence rising above the Chiana. It is a neat clean town, with "some handsome but deserted palaces." Pop. 2,600. It is the see of a bishop, and contains a cathedral, 3 churches, and 6 convents. It was the birth-place of Pietro Perugino.

CITTA'-DI-CASTELLO, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 27 m. NNW of Perugia, on the l. bank of the Upper Tiber. It is a well-built town, of 7,000 inhabitants, and contains several fine churches, rich in paintings, and a cathedral church, all of which, and particularly the latter, are fully noticed in Murray's Hand-book. The fair of C.—which once attracted merchants from all parts of Italy-has declined to a 2d or 3d rate gathering of mere provincial traders.—C. occupies the site of the ancient Tifernum Tiberinum.
CITTA-DUCALE, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo- Ultra 2da, 22 m. WNW of Aquila, and 5 m. E of Rieti, on the r. bank of the Velino. Pop. 3,331. It was nearly destroyed by an earthquake in 1703.
CITTA-NOVA, a town of Austria, in Illyria, in the circle of Pisino, at the embouchure of the Quieto, 36 m. SSW of Trieste, in N lat. 45° 18' 20". It has a small and secure port. Pop. 800.
CITTA-REALE, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo- Ultra 2da, 20 m. NE of Citta - Ducale, near the source of the Velino. Pop. 1,754.
CITTA-SAN-ANGELO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra 1ma, 24 m. SE of Teramo, 2 m. from the Adriatic coast. Pop. 5,994. It has an active trade in grain, oil, and wine.
CITTA-VECCHIA, a town of Austria, in Dalmatia, on the N coast of the island of Lesina, in the circle and 25 m. SE of Spalatro. Pop. 2,100.

CITTA-VECCHIA, or CITTA-NOTABILE, a town of Malta, situated on one of the highest points and near the centre of the island, 6 m. W of Valetta. It is the seat of the bishop of Malta, and contains a cathedral, and some magnificent but deserted buildings. bery. There are immense catacombs here, excavated in strata of soft tertiary limestone, and supposed to be the work of the early Christians. At 1 m. distance is the castle of Verdala, built in the 16th cent.
CITY-POINT, a port of entry in Prince George co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., on the S side of James river, at the confluence of the Appomatox, 33 m. SE of Richmond.
CITY-WEST, a village in Porter co., in the state of Iowa, on Lake Michigan.
CIUDAD-DE-LAS-CASAS. See CHIAPA-DELOS-ESPANOLES.

CIUDADELA, a town on the W coast of the island of Minorca, 24 m. WNW of Mahon. Pop. of the town and termino or district 7,000. It is surrounded by noble walls, 13 m. in circuit, with immense gateways. The harbour is a narrow inlet of the sea, with only 11 ft. water half-way up, at the head of which the town is built on the cliffs, with neatly finished terraces and batteries. The streets | Portuguese army on the 8th January, 1812. On the CIU are narrow and crooked, but contain some fine buildings. C. is the cap. of the island, and as such the residence of the captain-general, the bishop, and most of the nobility of the island. It is connected with the more important port of Mahon by a fine spacious road.

CIUDAD-REAL, a province of Spain, in New Castile, nearly corresponding to the ancient prov. of La Mancha. It is bounded on the N by Toledo; on the NE by Cuenca; on the E by Albacete; on the S by Jaen and Cordova; and on the W by Badajoz and Caceres. Its principal rivers are the Guadiana, the Tagus, the Xucar, the Xiguela, and the Jabalon. The prevailing character of the country is that of immense and highly elevated table-lands, covered with a parched soil, and but partially cultivated. On the S frontier rises the Sierra Morena; on the N, the mountains of Toledo; on the SE, the Sierra de Alcaraz. It is rich in minerals. The climate is intensely hot in summer, while it is rendered piercingly cold in winter by the keen winds which then prevail. The area has been estimated at 7,543 sq. m. The pop. in 1834 was 277,788. It is comprehended ecclesiastically in the diocese of Toledo; and in military affairs is dependent upon the captaingeneralship of New Castile. It is subdivided into 10 judicial partidos, viz.: Alcazar-de-San-Juan, Almaden, Almagov, Almodovar-del-Campo, C., Daimiel, Manzanares, Piedrabuena, Valdepenas, and Villanueva-de-los-Infantes. Its principal towns give name to the partidos.

CIUDAD-REAL, the cap. of the above prov. and of the judicial partido of the same name, is situated in a fertile tract between the Guadiana and Jabalon, 8 m. ENE of the confluence of these rivers, 70 m. SSE of Toledo, and 90 m. S of Madrid. Pop. 8,300. It is walled round. The streets are spacious, straight, and well-paved; and it contains a noble square, which is occasionally used as an arena for bull-fights and other public entertainments. Of the buildings worthy of note the chief are the churches, one of which is a handsome edifice; the work-house, a magnificent structure, founded by Cardinal Lorenzana, archbishop of Toledo, for the education and training of poor children; and the college. This town was formerly celebrated for its manufactures of cloth, leather, and gloves; and it still enjoys an active trade in wine, fruit, oil, and mules. An extensive cattle-fair is held once a-year.—C., formerly a place of considerable strength, and the cap. of La Mancha, was founded subsequent to the expulsion of the Moors from that prov. It was the head-quarters of the celebrated fraternity of the Santa-Hermandad founded in 1249 for the suppression of highway rob-
CIUDAD-REAL, a town of Venezuela, in Spanish Guayana, 201 m. SSE of Caracas, on the r. bank of the Orinoco. It was founded in 1759.

CIUDAD-RODRI'GO, a frontier town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 55 m. SW of Salamanca, in a fertile plain, near the r. bank of the Agueda, which is here crossed by a handsome bridge. Pop. 4,612. It is defended by a wall, a deep fosse, and a strong citadel, and has two suburbs. The streets are laid out with tolerable regularity. The principal buildings are the cathedral, a handsome Gothic edifice; the churches, of which there are several; the convents, and the college. It possesses manufactories of woollen fabrics, linen, leather, and soap, and a considerable trade in cattle.—This town, founded in the 13th cent., in the reign of Ferdinand II., occupies the site of the ancient Lancia-Transcudana of the Romans. In 1706 it was taken by the Portuguese; in 1810 by the French; and by the Anglooccasion of the latter conquest, the title of Duke of Ciudad-Rodrigo was conferred by the Spanish government upon the Duke of Wellington. The partido comprises 78 pueblos.
CIVEZZANO, a village of Tyrol, in the circle and 5 m. ENE of Trent, on an affluent of the Adige. Fairs are held here twice a-year.

CIVIDALE-DEL-FRIULI, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, delegation and 11 m. ENE of Udine, on the Nattisone, an affluent of the Isonzo. Pop. in 1837, 6,178. This town, the Forum Julii of the Romans, is of great antiquity, and is still surrounded by old walls and a ditch. It has three annual fairs. CIVITA,—an Italian word signifying 'town.' For some names beginning with this word, see CITTA, or the second member of the compound name. The name C. and its derivations, seem frequently to indicate the remains of ancient towns and cities.
CIVITA-CAMPOMORANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, district and 9 m. W of Larino. Pop. 3,189.

CIVITA-CASTELLANA, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 18 m. SE of Viterbo, and 35 m. N of Rome. It is a fortified town, well situated on a detached eminence, and its citadel is used as a state-prison. Pop. 3,000. It is the see of the bishop of Civita-Castellana-Orte-et-Gallese. The road from Florence to Rome runs through the principal street, and immediately before entering the gate is carried by a lofty viaduct over the Triglia. The site of C. is altogether romantic, presenting in ravine, rock, river, bridge, grey walls, and tumbling torrents, "all those things which inhabitants disregard and travellers love." It is supposed by some antiquaries to occupy the site of the ancient Fescennium; by others, that of Falerii.-In 1798, the French, under Macdonald, defeated the Neapolitans, under Mack, at this place.

CIVITA-LAVIGNA, a town of the Papal states, 18 m. SSE of Rome, beautifully placed on a spur attached to the W point of the Alban mountain. Pop. 800. Being constructed of old dark-coloured bricks of peperino, with narrow gloomy streets, it has a singular air of antiquity.
CIVITA-NOVA, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 12 m. E of Macerata.
CIVITAQUANA, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo- Ultra 1ma, 9 m. S of Penne. Pop. 1,311.
CIVITATE, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 14 m. SE of Bergamo, on the r. bank of the Oglio. Pop. 1,800. It has an annual fair.

CIVITA-VECCHIA, a delegazione or administrative division of the Papal states; bounded on the NW, N, and NE by the deleg. of Viterbo; on the SE by the comarca of Rome; on the SW by the Mediterranean. It is one of the smallest of the Roman provinces, having a length from NW to SE of only 30 m., and a breadth from NE to SW of 20 m, with a superficial area of 412 Italian sq. m.; and a pop. in 1833 of only 19,601. Its W section is low and marshy in surface; but towards the E it is covered by ramifications of the Tuscan Sub-Apennines. The principal rivers are the Arrone, Marta, and Mignone. It is subdivided into 4 governi, viz.: C., Corneto, Manziana, and Monte-Romano.

CIVITA-VECCHIA, the capital of the above delegation, the ancient Centum Cellae, is situated on the coast, 36 m. NW of Rome. It is a well-built and fortified town, with a good harbour, and light-house in N lat. 42° 5′ 24", E long. 11° 44′ 4". It is the see of the bishop of Civita-Vecchia-Porto-et-Santa-Rufina, having been created an episcopal see in 1825. The pop. of the town is about 8,000; but is rapidly increasing in consequence of the steamers between CLA Marseilles and Naples regularly touching here 3 times a-month each way. The exports from C. chiefly consist of wood, wool, cheese, potash, and alum. The latter is obtained from extensive government works at Tolfa, about 15 m. distant. The imports consist of cotton, woollen, silk, and linen stuffs, coffee, sugar, cocoa, salt, salted fish, glass, and earthenware. The port is a free one. The harbour has from 14 to 18 ft. of water; but ships may anchor in deeper water inside the outer mole. Criminals condemned to the galleys are kept at work here. Their number is said to exceed 1,000. The surrounding country is desolate and uncultivated.
CIVITELLA, a village in the Papal states, in the deleg. and 15 m. S of Forli, on the r. bank of the Ronco.—Also a v. in the comarca and 30 m. E of Rome.
CIVITELLA-D'AGLIANO, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 13 m. N of Viterbo, on the Chiaro, near its junction with the Tiber.
CIVITELLA-MESSER-RAIMONDO, a village of Naples, in Abruzzo-Citra, district and 18 m. SW of Lanciano. Pop. 1,500.
CIVITELLA-ROVETO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra 2da, district and 8 m. S of Avezzano, near the Liri. Pop. 1,388.
CIVITELLA-DEL-TRONTO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Citra, district and 8 m. N of Teramo, near the r. bank of the Salinello. Pop. 5,703. It occupies the summit of a hill, and is well-fortified.
CIVITELLA-DEL-VESCOVO, a town of Naples, in the comp. and 9 m. S of Arezzo, at an alt. of 1,710 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 654.

CIVRAY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Bleré. Pop. 1,029. -Also an arrond., cant., and com., in the dep. of La Vienne. The arrond., comprising the 5 cant. of Availles, Charroux, C., Couhé, and Gençais, has an area of 117,700 hectares. Pop. in 1841, 46,710; in 1846, 49,382.—The cant., comprising 12 com., had a pop. of 11,155 in 1831.—The com. and town are on on the r. bank of the Charente, 30 m. S of Poitiers. Pop. in 1821, 2,083; in 1841, 2,202.
CIVRO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. of Valteline, 14 m. W of Sondrio. Pop. 2,600.

CLACKMANNAN, a parish and town in the co. of the same name. The superficial area of the p. is about 7,000 acres. It is watered by the two Devons. Pop. of the town and parish in 1801, 2,961; in 1841, 5,145.—The town of C. is situated on an eminence gently rising out of a plain to the height of 190 ft. above the level of the Forth, 2 m. E of Alloa. The principal street of the town is broad and spacious; but many of the houses are mean. Pop. 1,077. A small harbour is formed by the confluence of the S Devon with the Forth. Its mean depth of water is 10 ft. at the usual shipping place, and 20 ft. at the mouth of the harbour.

CLACKMANNANSHIRE, a county of Scotland, bounded on the S and W by the frith of Forth, which separates it from Stirlingshire; and on the N and E by Perthshire, excepting at one point where it adjoins the county of Fife. This county contains only 4 parishes, and about 30,720 English acres, of which 22,000 are cultivated, 5,000 uncultivated, and 3,720 nearly unprofitable. Pop. in 1801, 10,858; in 1831, 14,729; in 1841, 19,116, being an increase of 29.7 per cent. on that of 1831. Inhabited houses in 1841, 3,593. It is conjoined with the shire of Kinross, and the two parishes of Tulliallan and Culross, in returning a member to parliament. Parliamentary constituency of the whole in 1842-3, 1,413. The surface of this co. rises gradually from the shores of the frith to the Ochil hills, the highest of which, Bencloch, is in the p. of Tillicoultry. On the banks of the Forth | 9,474. Its cap. is Tazewell.—Also a village in Monthe country is flat and rich; and the Ochils afford pasturage for sheep not surpassed in Scotland. To the S of the Ochils lies the beautiful vale of Devon. 497. Betwixt this vale and the foot of the mountains, the soil is in general light and of a fine quality, but not deep. To the S of the Devon the surface begins to rise, and the soil possesses much of that clay, scarcely penetrable by water, which is so generally found in districts containing coal and freestone. From this ridge, the country descends gradually to the shores of the Forth, along the whole of which is a level tract consisting of rich carse lands, part of the carse of Stirling. The climate of this district is various: snow seldom lies on the low grounds of Logie, or in the vale of Devon; although the case is very different upon the hills. The rental of the co. in 1811 was £32,047. The annual value of assessed property in 1815, was £37,978; in 1823, £52,923. In this county and its neighbourhood a considerable number of small feus are held in perpetuity. In the carse the farms are small, containing only about 80 or 100 acres each.—The Devon, from its source in the p. of Blackford in Perthshire, to where it falls into the Forth, at the village of Cambus, not more than 6 m. in a straight line from its source, has a course of 26 m. In the lower part of the county is another river called the S Devon, and sometimes the Black Devon, from the gloomy density of its waters. This stream rises in the hills of Saline, in the county of Fife, and flowing W, in a direction nearly parallel to the Devon, falls into the Forth in the parish of Clackmannan.—There is rather a deficiency of wood in the county. The ancient forest of C. has long since disappeared. Attempts have been made to cover the hills to a considerable height, which, in time, may probably succeed, but the progress of vegetation is here remarkably slow. C. abounds with coal; freestone and granite are also abundant. In the Ochils have been wrought at various times silver, lead, copper, cobalt, ironstone, and antimony. Many rich specimens of septaria have also been found.
CLACTON (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes in Essex. The former is 14 m. ESE of Colchester. Area 4,170 acres. Pop. 1,296. The latter, 12 m. ESE of Colchester, has an area of 3,110 acres. Pop. 547.
CLADDAGH, a village in the p. of Kilmacteigue, co. Sligo. Pop. 150.

CLADY, a village in the p. of Urney, co. Tyrone, on the r. bank of the Finn, 3 m. SW by S of Strabane. Pop. 219.—Also a hamlet in co. Londonderry, 13 m. WNW of Portglenone.—Also a rivulet in co. Londonderry, rising on the E side of the Carntopher Mountains, passing Maghera, and falling into the Lower Bann, after a course of 10 m.—Also a rivulet in co. Donegal, issuing from Lough Nacung, and running 6 m. W to Guidore bay.
CLADY, or CUMBER-CLADY, a village in the p. of Upper Cumber, co. Londonderry, on the r. bank of the Faughan, 8 m. W by N of Dungiven. Pop. 191.
CLAGENFURT. See KLAGENFURT.
CLAGGAN, a headland and bay in co. Galway, opposite Innisboffin. The bay is 14 m. wide at the entrance, and penetrates the land <math>2\frac{3}{4}</math> m. ESE. It has good anchorage and shelter.

CLAIBORNE, a county in the SW part of the state of Mississippi, U. S., on the Mississippi river, and skirted on the NW by the Big Black river. Area 500 sq. m. Its cap. is Port Gibson. Pop. 13,078.—Also a parish in the state of Louisiana, bounded by Red river on the W, and Arkansas on the N. Pop. 6,185. Its cap. is Overton.—Also a county in the NE part of the state of Tennessee, intersected by Powell's river, and Clinch river. Pop. 41° and 42° S. It is about 10 m. long, and 3 m. CLA roe co., in Alabama, 138 m. S of Tuscaloosa.—Also a township in Union co., in the state of Ohio. Pop.
CLAIBORNEVILLE, a village in Yazu co., in Mississippi, 57 m. N of Jackson.
CLAIFE, a township in the p. of Hawkeshead, Lancashire, 2 m. SE of Hawkeshead. Area 5,310 acres. Pop. 541.

CLAIN, a river of France, rising to the NW of Confolens in Charente, and flowing from S to N past Vivonne and Poitiers, to the Vienne, which it joins 3 m. S of Chatellerault, after a course of 80 m. Its principal affluents on the r. are the Clouère and the Miausom, on the l. the Vonne and the Auzance.
CLAINES, a parish and village in Worcestershire, 2½ m. N of Worcester. Area of p. 4,600 acres. Pop. 6,395.

CLAIR (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Manche, arrond. of Saint-Lo. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,624; in 1841, 10,189.—The com., 6 m. NE of Saint-Lo, had a pop. of 722 in 1841.—Also a hamlet in the dep. of Rhone, com. of La-Croix-Rousse, Pop. 2,589.

CLAIR (SAINT), a county in the NE part of the state of Alabama, U. S., bounded by the Coosa on the E and SE, and by Wills creek on the NE. Area 840 sq. m. Pop. in 1841, 5,638. The cap. is Asheville.-Also a county in the E part of the state of Michigan, intersected by Black, Pine, and Belle rivers, and bounded by Clair strait on the E. Area 930 sq. m. Pop. 4,606. The cap. is St. Clair, on the S side of Pine river. Pop. 413.—Also a county in the SW of Illinois, bounded by the Mississippi on the W, and intersected by the Kaskaskia. Area 648 sq. m. Pop. 13,631. The cap. is Belleville.—Also a county in the SW of the state of Missouri, on the Osage river. -Also a township in Bedford co., in Pennsylvania, 113 m. W of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,488.—Also a township in Butler co., in the state of Ohio, on the W side of Great Miami river. Pop. 1,174.—Also a township in Columbiana co., in Ohio, 11 m. SE of New Lisbon. Pop. 1,739.

CLAIR (SAINT), a river of North America issuing from Lake Huron, and flowing by a number of arms into the NE side of Lake Saint Clair. It is about 40 m. in length, and a 1 m. wide, with an average depth of 50 ft., a current of 3 m. an hour, and a fall of 13 ft. from lake to lake.

CLAIR (LAKE SAINT), a small lake between the state of Michigan, U. S., on the W, and Upper Canada on the E, situated between Lake Erie on the S, and Lake Huron on the N; and connected with the former by the Detroit river, issuing from its SW angle, and with the latter by the Saint Clair. It is 30 m. in length, from N to S; 24 m. in breadth; and 90 m. in circumf. Its average depth. is 20 ft. Its banks are alluvial, and have an elevation of only about 20 ft, above the water, while there are no hills in the immediate interior, and the entire fall between Lakes Huron and Erie is only 30 ft. It is an exception to the other lakes in this region of America in the comparative shallowness of its waters, except in the channel of the current which flows throughout downwards to Lake Erie, and which channel is perpetually shifting under the action of storms, so as to require a frequent survey for the direction of the pilot. It receives, besides the waters of the St. Clair, those of two considerable rivers from the E, the Thames and the Great Bear. It is frozen over every year from December to February.

CLAIR (LAKE SAINT), a lake in Van Diemen's Land, in the unexplored and elevated plains towards the centre of the island, and between the parallels of wide, and is surrounded by lofty mountains. The river Derwent has its head-stream in this lake.
CLAIR-SUR-EPTE (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Magny, 16 m. N of Mantes, on the l. bank of the Epte. Pop. 600.
CLAIR-SUR-GALAURE (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Roybon. Pop. 564.
CLAIRA, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Rivesaltes. Pop. 1,049.

CLAIRAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Tonneins, on the r. bank of the Lot, 16 m. SE of Marmande. Pop. in 1841, 4,842. White wines and tobacco are the staple produce of the district, which is chiefly inhabited by Protestants.
CLAIREGOUTTE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Champagney, 8 m. E of Lure. Pop. 600.
CLAIRFONTAINE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of La Capelle, 10 m. NNE of Vervins. Pop. 1,426.

CLAIRVAUX, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aube, cant. and 6 m. SSE of Bar-sur-Aube, on the l. bank of the Aube. Pop. in 1831, 1,950. The celebrated Benedictine Abbey of C., founded here in 1115 by Hugues, count of Troyes, and Etienne, abbé of Citeaux, and of which Saint Bernard was the first abbé, is now converted into a prison and house-ofcorrection, in which in 1835 there were 1,270 prisoners.—Also a com. and town in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Marcillac, 8 m. NW of Rodez. Pop. 2,166. -Also a cant., com., and town, in the dep. of Jura. The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1841, 7,759. The com., 13 m. SE of Lons-le-Saulnier, on the l. bank of the Drouenne, had a pop. of 1,306 in 1841.
CLAIS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Vif, near the l. bank of the Romanche, 6 m. SSW of Grenoble. Pop. 1,659.

CLAISE, a river of France, rising 4 m. SW of Chateauroux in Indre, and flowing W past Mezieren-Brenne and Martizay, and then NW past Preuilly to the Creuse, which it joins on the r. bank, 13 m. S of La Haye, after a course of 48 m.

CLAMART-SOUS-MEUDON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Sceaux, 5 m. SW of Paris. Pop. 1,229. The railway from Paris to Versailles, by the l. bank of the river, has a station here.

CLAME'CY, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Nièvre.—The arrond. has an area of 147,251 hectares, and a pop. in 1841 of 70,381. It comprises the 6 cant. of Brinonles-Allemands, C., Corbigny, Lormes, Tannay, and Varzy.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. 13,360 in 1831.—The com. and town are on the l. bank of the Yome, at the junction of the Beuvron, 30 m. E of Cône, 40 m. NNE of Nevers. Pop. in 1821, 5,311; in 1841, 5,734. It contains several old churches, and some relics of its old and strong castle. It has manufactures of coarse woollens, and of earthenware; and possesses fulling-mills, dye-works, and tanneries.
CLAN, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Vienne, com. of Jaulnay. Pop. 1,482.
CLANABOROUGH, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. SE of Bow. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 69.
CLANDON (East and West), two parishes in Surrey. The former is 41 m. ENE of Guildford. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 293. The latter is 3\frac{1}{2} m. ENE of Guildford. Area 990 acres. Pop. 407.
CLANE, a parish and village in co. Kildare. Area of p. 4,663 acres. Pop. 2,160. The v. is on the l. bank of the Liffey, 61 m. SW by W of Celbridge. Pop. 335. CLA
CLANFIELD, a parish in Oxfordshire, 5 m. ENE of Lechlade. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 584.—Also a p. in Hants, 6 m. SW of Petersfield. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 239.

CLAPHAM, a parish in Bedfordshire, 21 m. NW of Bedford, on the N bank of the Ouse. Area 1,490 acres. Pop. 370.—Also a p. in Surrey, 4 m. SSW of St. Paul's. Area 1,070 acres. Pop. 12,106. It is within the limits of the London police, and is one of the handsomest suburbs of London.-Also a p. in Sussex, 6 m. SW of Steyning. Area 1,920 acres. Pop. 262.—Also a p. in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NW of Settle. Area 24,340 acres. Pop. 1,853.

CLAPPERTON ISLAND, an island in the Arctic ocean, in Darnley bay, in N lat. 69° 41½, 14 m. SE of Cape Lyon. Like the neighbouring coast, it is composed of limestone. It was discovered by Franklin, and named by him in honour of the undaunted explorer of Central Africa.
CLAP'S ISLAND, or Breaker's Island, a small island about 7 leagues SE by E of Java-head, at the W end of the island of Java, and 3\frac{1}{2} leagues W by S of Trowers' island.

CLAPTON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 41 m. NE of North Leach. Area 700 acres. Pop. 117.—Also a p. in Northamptonshire, 41 m. E by N of Thrapston. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 119.-Also a p. in Somersetshire, 6 m. W of Bristol. Area 980 acres. Pop. 148.—Also a tything in the p. of Midsummer- Norton, 10 m. SW of Bath. Pop. 140.
CLAPVILLE, a village in Worcester co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 50 m. W by S of Boston, on the upper part of Quinebaug river.

CLAR (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Gers, arrond. of Lectoure.—The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. 8,523.—The com., 9 m. ESE of Lectoure, near the l. bank of the Ratz, had a pop. of 1,638 in 1841.
CLARA, a town in the p. of Kilbride-Langan, in co. Leinster, 41 m. SW of Kilbeggan. Pop. 1,155. -Also a p. in co. Kilkenny, 31 m. E by N of Kilkenny. Area 3,210 acres. Pop. 663.
CLARA, an island in the Mergui archipelago, off the coast of Tenasserim, in N lat. 11° 30', and 97° 35' E long.
CLARA. See KLAR.
CLARA-BRIDGE, a village in the p. of Stradbally, in the co. of Galway, 3 m. S by E of Oranmore. Pop. 200.
CLARA (Point), a projection of the coast at the entrance of the Gabun river, in Upper Guinea, in N lat. 0° 30½, E long. 9° 20½.

CLARA (SANTA), or VILLA-CLARA, a jurisdiction and town in the island of Cuba, in the dep. of Del Centro, 50 m. NNW of Trinidad. Pop. in 1841, 6,132, of whom 3,355 were whites, 1,891 free coloured, and 886 slaves. Sugar is extensively cultivated in this district.
CLARA (Santa), an islet in the gulf of Guyaquil, off the coast of Ecuador, in S lat. 3° 15', 14 m. SSW of Puna.

CLARA (SANTA), a small mission or settlement in Upper California, in N lat. 37° 25', 20 m. SSE of San Francisco. It is situated in an extensive plain, the soil of which is a rich black mould, producing good crops of wheat, maize, pease, and beans, and pasturing large flocks of cattle and sheep. When Kotzebue visited this mission in 1826, it contained 1,500 male Indians, of whom about one-half were married, and all these men, he adds, "are governed by 3 monks, and guarded by 4 soldiers and a subaltern officer."

CLARAC, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, arrond. of Pau.—The cant., comprising 15 com., had a pop. in 1841 of 12,445.—The com., 12 m. SE of Pau, has a a pop. of 330.
CLARATOMBA. See Mogila.
CLARBESTON, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 5 m. NNW of Narberth. Pop. 244.

CLARE, a parish and market-town in Suffolk, 15 m. SSW of Bury-St.-Edmond's, on the N bank of the Stour. Area of p. 3,410 acres. Pop. 1,700.—The town is situated on the Stour, which separates it from Essex. It is a place of considerable antiquity, and has a fine old church.

CLARE, a maritime county of Ireland, forming the NW district of the prov. of Munster. It is bounded on the N by Galway bay and the co. of Galway; on the NE by Galway; on the E by Tipperary; on the SE by Limerick; on the S by Limerick and Kerry; and on the W by the Atlantic ocean. The Shannon, from the broadest part of its expansion in Lough Derg, down to its embouchure on the Atlantic ocean, forms all the E and S boundary-line. Its greatest length from E to W is about 52 m.; its greatest breadth from N to S is about 33 m.; and its area comprises 455,009 acres of arable land, 296,033 of uncultivated land, 8,304 of plantations, 728 of towns, and 67,920 of water, -in all 827,994 acres. -The surface is exceedingly irregular. In a general view, a grand group of mountains covers an area of about 150 sq. m. in the NE and E,—a great champaign district forms the centre of the co., - and a vast district of about 400 sq. m., between the champaign country and the Atlantic, consists, for the most part, of high grounds, which are now mountainous, now a mass of flattened moorish hills. The chief portion of the mountain-group in the E of the co. consists of the Slieve-Baughta mountains, which raise their principal summits to the alt. of from 2,000 to 2,500 ft.; and the chief single mass in the W region is Callan mountain, — a huge, broad-based, manysummited, and many-spurred mass.—The extent of coast on Galway bay is 9 m.; on what is called the South sound, 13 m.; the extent directly upon the Atlantic is about 38 m.; and on the Shannon, to the termination of the estuary at Limerick, about 48 m. The N coast abounds in coves, creeks, and small natural harbours. The coast from Black-head to Loophead is prevailingly bold, rocky, and iron-bound. Its cliffs average about 100 ft. in height; but frequently rise to 400, and occasionally to near 1,000 ft. The coast on the Shannon varies in character from bold and precipitous to low and meadowy. The estuary of the Fergus opens from the Shannon with a width of 5 m.; penetrates the county northward to the extent of 75 m.; and is sprinkled over with numerous islands and islets. Waters.] The river Fergus runs through the lakes of Inchiquin, Tedane, Dromore, and Ballyally; flows past the town of Ennis; and begins to expand into its estuary a little below the v. of Clare. The river Ougarnee collects its head-waters in Lough-Breedy, and pursues a S course past Six-mile-Bridge to the Shannon, opposite the embouchure of the Maig. The Innistymon or Forsett circles round Mount Callan; falls over a large ledge of rocks at Innistymon; and careers thence into the head of Liscanor bay, forming altogether a length of course of about 16 or 17 m. Very many rivulets and brooks traverse almost every part of the county, and generally take their names from the villages or other remarkable localities which they wash. Lakes and loughlets are so numerous, that upwards of 100 figure in topographical nomenclature; and several, such as those of Grany, O'Grady, Tedane, Inchiquin, Cloonlea, and Inishcronan, are of considerable size. Turloughs, or temporary or periodical lakes, formed either by the accumulation of surface-water, or the forcing up of subterranean water II. by the flow of high absorbed rills, are numerous; and mineral springs, chiefly chalybeate, abound. Geology.] Three fields of schistose rocks, chiefly argillaceous, occur in the E mountain division of the county. An old red sandstone formation, partly stratified and partly conglomerate, completely surrounds and insulates all the schistose fields. The Slieve- Baughta mountain-region has been not very inaccurately, though rather loosely, described as "consisting of a nucleus of clay-slate," only the nucleus is comparatively a large one, "supporting flanks of sandstone, intruded through a break, in the surrounding limestone plain, in the same manner as the Slieve-Bloom range on the opposite bank of the Shannon."-The limestone formation, including not only carboniferous or mountain limestone, but the lower limestone calp or black shale series, and the upper limestone, occupies all the N part of the county for 6 or 7 m. S of Black-head, -all the central, champaign district down to the Shannon,-and all the area to the E, not occupied by the formations already named .- A coal formation, including the shales, sandstones, and trappean veins and nodules which so generally accompany coal, occupies all the great tract W from the limestone to the Atlantic, comprehending an area, at the lowest estimate, of 600 or 650 sq. m. —A very fine black marble has been raised at Craggliath, near Ennis. -Limestone, of very various texture, yet generally rich enough in carbonate to be an excellent manure, might be worked in almost every part of the great limestone district. Sandstone flags of the coal formation, curiously separated by serpentine insertions between the layers, are raised a few miles from Kilrush. Sandstone slates, similar to the former, but so much thinner that a ton will slate about a square of 100 ft., are raised at Ballagh, Glenomera, and various places in the W; and the Broadford and Killaloe slates are regarded as nearly equal to the best Welsh slates. Workable coal is not known to exist, except in a very limited district on the Shannon, about 11 m. above Kilrush. A vein of purple fluor spar, similar to that worked into ornaments in Derbyshire, occurs at Doolen. Copper pyrites occur at Doolen and other localities in Burren; and an attempt was at one time made in that barony to work a copper-mine. Lead ore, in some instances apparently rich and abundant, occurs near Tulla, and in Glendree, near Feacle. Iron ore, variously in the stone and in the ochreish state, has been found at Class, on Goat island, on the shore of Liscanor bay, and near Pooldagh on the Mal bay coast. Manganese has been found at the spa of Fierd, on the shore near Cross. Antimony, potter's clays, valuable ochres, and other useful minerals have also been observed. Climate and Vegetation.] The strong gales from the Atlantic are unfriendly to the growth of timber, and their influence is felt upwards of 50 m. from the sea.
